description: In a VSF stack, the switch may fail to log events for the matching access-list entries. ACL functionality is not impacted; access-list entries are applied properly and only the logging is incorrectly generated.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what is the caveat related to ACLs in a VSF stack?

Feature Caveat: ACLs - In a VSF stack, the switch may fail to log events for matching access-list entries. ACL functionality is not impacted; access-list entries are applied properly and only the logging is incorrectly generated.

description: If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for Activate?

Feature Caveat: Activate - If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

description: When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for Central?

Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

description: When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. For new certificate names, only alphanumeric characters, dots, dashes, and underscores are allowed.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for Certificates?

Feature Caveat: Certificates - When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. For new certificate names, only alphanumeric characters, dots, dashes, and underscores are allowed.

description: Policies containing both MAC and IPv6 classes are not allowed.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for Classifiers?
Feature Caveat: Classifiers - Policies containing both MAC and IPv6 classes are not allowed.

description: For secure classifier policy modifications, HPE Aruba Networking strongly encourages a three-step process: bring down the port, make the modifications, and then bring the port back up.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what limitation is mentioned for Classifiers in HPE Aruba AOS-CX 10.17.0001?
For secure classifier policy modifications, HPE Aruba Networking strongly encourages a three-step process: bring down the port, make the modifications, and then bring the port back up.

description: The Client IP tracker tracks IP addresses until IP source lockdown is applied. After lockdown, only valid binding table entries are tracked. Already tracked IPs remain active through ARP probes, which use Layer 2 packets.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for Client IP Tracker?

Feature Caveat: Client IP Tracker - The Client IP tracker tracks IP addresses until IP source lockdown is applied. After lockdown, only valid binding table entries are tracked. Already tracked IPs remain active through ARP probes, which use Layer 2 packets.

description: No other checkpoint besides "startup-configuration" gets migrated during the upgrade process.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for CMF?
CMF - No other checkpoint besides 'startup-configuration' gets migrated during the upgrade process.

description: When downloading a hot-patch file using the switch WebUI, log messages may incorrectly state that the file is added to the database with a missing status. This is a temporary state, and will correctly change to Not applied once the download is completed.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for Hot Patch?

Feature Caveat: Hot Patch - When downloading a hot-patch file using the switch WebUI, log messages may incorrectly state that the file is added to the database with a missing status. This is a temporary state, and will correctly change to Not applied once the download is completed.

feature: HPE Aruba Networking CX Mobile App
description: VSF stack formation is blocked when there are reserved autojoin interfaces (25, 26, 49, 50) in the stack topology.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for HPE Aruba Networking CX Mobile App?
Feature Caveat: HPE Aruba Networking CX Mobile App - VSF stack formation is blocked when there are reserved autojoin interfaces (25, 26, 49, 50) in the stack topology.

description: The switch may only forward packets at a rate of 100 packets per second (pps) if they trigger an ICMP redirect.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, for HPE Aruba AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for ICMP Redirect?
Feature Caveat: ICMP Redirect - The switch may only forward packets at a rate of 100 packets per second (pps) if they trigger an ICMP redirect.

description: Do not use reserved ports or ports used by other applications/services for different services. When two services use the same port, unexpected behaviors may occur. Best practice is to assign a unique port for each service across the system.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for IP-SLA?

Feature Caveat: IP-SLA - Do not use reserved ports or ports used by other applications/services for different services. When two services use the same port, unexpected behaviors may occur. Best practice is to assign a unique port for each service across the system.

description: The IP source-lockdown feature allows client traffic through the switch only if their MAC and IP addresses are in the binding database. VLAN-level configuration overrides port-level configuration only when the port belongs to a single VLAN; otherwise, VLAN-level configuration is not applied.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for IP Source Lockdown?

Feature Caveat: IP Source Lockdown - The IP source-lockdown feature allows client traffic through the switch only if their MAC and IP addresses are in the binding database. VLAN-level configuration overrides port-level configuration only when the port belongs to a single VLAN; otherwise, VLAN-level configuration is not applied.

description: MACsec works between a CX device and a Windows VM running AnyConnect with AES-128 cipher. AnyConnect does not support AES-256 in the NAM module (works only for the VPN module). NOTE MACsec is supported CX 6200M switch models only (R8Q67A, R8Q68A, R8Q69A, R8Q70A, and R8Q71A, R8V08A, R8V09A, R8V10A, R8V11A, and R8V12A).

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, for AOS-CX 6200 version 10.17.0001, what is the caveat regarding MACsec?

AnyConnect does not support AES-256 in the NAM module (works only for the VPN module). description: When using Cisco AnyConnect as a dot1x supplicant, configure the CAK length to 16 under dot1x-authenticator mode. NOTE MACsec is supported CX 6200M switch models only (R8Q67A, R8Q68A, R8Q69A, R8Q70A, and R8Q71A, R8V08A, R8V09A, R8V10A, R8V11A, and R8V12A).

description: Ensure the cipher suite GCM-AES-128 is configured when AOS-CX is acting as a key server. By default, AOS-CX uses the more secure cipher suite GCM-AES-XPN-256 for establishing MACsec secure links. However, Comware/PVOS does not support the XPN cipher suite.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?

Feature Caveat: MACsec - Ensure the cipher suite GCM-AES-128 is configured when acting as a key server. By default, AOS-CX uses the more secure cipher suite GCM-AES-XPN-256 for establishing MACsec secure links. However, Comware/PVOS does not support the XPN cipher suite.

description: MACsec uses a software-based implementation to track start and stop times for secure channels and secure associations. As the implementation is software-based, the stop times for MACsec secure channel and secure associations are only updated when they are deleted and therefore never updated in the output of the show macsec status detailed command. NOTE MACsec is supported CX 6200M switch models only (R8Q67A, R8Q68A, R8Q69A, R8Q70A, and R8Q71A, R8V08A, R8V09A, R8V10A, R8V11A, and R8V12A).

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, for AOS-CX 6200 version 10.17.0001, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?

Feature Caveat: MACsec - The implementation of MACsec on the 6200 AOS-CX uses software to track start and stop times for secure channels and associations. These stop times are only updated when the secure channel or association is deleted, meaning they are never reflected in the output of the show macsec status detailed command. Note that MACsec is supported on CX 6200M switch models only (R8Q67A, R8Q68A, R8Q69A, R8Q70A, and R8Q71A, R8V08A, R8V09A, R8V10A, R8V11A, and R8V12A).

description: In an environment with Cisco and FlexFabric or H3C devices, do not update confidentiality-offset on the live channel. There can be complete traffic loss for an extended period on the MACsec channel when confidentiality-offset is updated on both ends. NOTE MACsec is supported CX 6200M switch models only (R8Q67A, R8Q68A, R8Q69A, R8Q70A, and R8Q71A, R8V08A, R8V09A, R8V10A, R8V11A, and R8V12A).

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, for AOS-CX 6200 version 10.17.0001, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?

description: In an environment with Juniper, configure no include-sci-tag under MACsec policy for data traffic forwarding. NOTE MACsec is supported CX 6200M switch models only (R8Q67A, R8Q68A, R8Q69A, R8Q70A, and R8Q71A, R8V08A, R8V09A, R8V10A, R8V11A, and R8V12A).

description: In an environment with Juniper devices, issue the command no include-sci-tag in a macsec policy context for data traffic forwarding.

Feature Caveat: MPLS - In an environment with Juniper devices, issue the command no include-sci-tag in a macsec policy context for data traffic forwarding.

description: DHCP Snooping (v4/v6) should not be enabled on the UBT client VLANs in either vlan extend and local vlan mode.

Feature Caveat: UBT/DHCP-Snooping - DHCP Snooping (v4/v6) should not be enabled on the UBT client VLANs in either vlan extend and local vlan mode.

description: Use standalone or cluster-specific IP addresses for primary and backup controllers, ensuring they are not from the same cluster. For the primary controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the primary cluster. For the backup controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the backup cluster. Do not configure both primary and backup controller IP addresses from the same cluster.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?

Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - Use standalone or cluster-specific IP addresses for primary and backup controllers, ensuring they are not from the same cluster. For the primary controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the primary cluster. For the backup controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the backup cluster. Do not configure both primary and backup controller IP addresses from the same cluster.

description: The switch does not support double encapsulation. A packet can be encapsulated with either L2GRE (UBT) or VXLAN, but not both. The network admin should decide the tunneling type to be used, and then plan the configuration accordingly.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?

Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - The switch does not support double encapsulation. A packet can be encapsulated with either L2GRE (UBT) or VXLAN, but not both. The network admin should decide the tunneling type to be used, and then plan the configuration accordingly.

description: If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what additional caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?

Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

description: Symptom: When Proxy ARP is configured, then the switch is responding to ARP request without validating ARP SRC IP. As a result, even though there is no reachability for ARP SRC IP in the switch, still it responds for ARP requests. Scenario: When an ARP request is received on an interface with Proxy ARP, then ARP response is sent without validating the ARP SRC IP. This is when there is a route to reach the destination but there is no reachability to ARP SRC IP.
symptom: When Proxy ARP is configured, then the switch is responding to ARP request without validating ARP SRC IP. As a result, even though there is no reachability for ARP SRC IP in the switch, still it responds for ARP requests.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what issue was resolved in ARP Bug 373811?

ARP (Bug ID 373811): Symptom: When Proxy ARP is configured, the switch responds to ARP requests without validating the ARP source IP. As a result, even though there is no reachability for the ARP source IP in the switch, it still responds to ARP requests. Scenario: When an ARP request is received on an interface with Proxy ARP, the switch sends an ARP response without validating the ARP source IP. This occurs when there is a route to reach the destination but no reachability to the ARP source IP.

description: Symptom: Customers see frequent log entries: HTTP <MAC-ADDRESS> - Invalid packet length or headers during normal HTTP traffic, even when no real packet corruption exists. No functional impact; just log noise. Scenario: This issue occurs on legitimate TCP packets to port 80 with no payload (ACK, SYN/ACK, FIN, keep-alive, window updates). Previously these were funneled into an error path (or indistinguishable from malformed cases), producing repeated ERR logs under moderate web or update traffic. Workaround: Disable the HTTP user-agent in device fingerprinting configuration.

description: Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Because of a flaw in the way that port MAC addresses are assigned in the swith, switch #1's port 1/1/2 will be assigned the identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two 6200, 6300, 6300L, 6400, 8100, or 8360 switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or don't use both of the ports with the overlap. Neither of those options is a feasible solution for a customer. Prior to the fix provided here, there is no explicit workaround for this issue.

description: Symptom: When wireless clients roam between access points connected to the same switch, they lose connectivity and receive an APIPA address. Connectivity is only restored after the MAC-age timer expires. Scenario: When a client is authenticated with a VLAN on a secure port, the VLAN is added as an ingress-only VLAN on other secure ports, but the MSTP state for those ports remains in blocked state instead of forwarding state, preventing seamless client movement. Workaround: Reduce the MAC-age timer to minimize the impact. or configure AP ports in device-mode instead of client-mode: interface 1/1/X port-access client-limit 1 port-access client-mode device.

description: Symptom: After a VSF system boot, isolated ports connected to clients experience complete traffic loss. Scenario: This issue can occur in a deployment with the following conditions: VSF has PVLAN configurations with an uplink LAG as promiscuous port. The LAG is made up of ports from VSF member 1 and member 2. For example, lag 1 with member ports 1/1/1 and 2/1/1 is the PVLAN promiscuous port. There are some of clients in a PVLAN- isolated VLAN connected to the VSF member 1 and member 2 physical ports.• After a VSF boot system, some of the clients are no longer reachable from when pinging through the uplink promiscuous port LAG• Workaround: On the LAG interface, use the command no private-VLAN port-type promiscuous to remove the promiscuous port type and then re-configure the setting with the command private-vlan port-type promiscuous.

There are some of clients in a PVLAN- isolated VLAN connected to the VSF member 1 and member 2 physical ports.• After a VSF boot system, some of the clients are no longer reachable from when pinging through the uplink promiscuous port LAG• Workaround: On the LAG interface, use the command no private-VLAN port-type promiscuous to remove the promiscuous port type and then re-configure the setting with the command private-vlan port-type promiscuous.

description: Symptom: The customer could see a system reboot on the VSF member with the reboot reason as critical service fault. The stack experienced a reboot due to failure of stack members to exchange heartbeat messages. Scenario: The customer could see this problem when they have a VSF stack device on the 6200 V1 platform switches. This issue is not reproducible consistently. Customer has seen this only once till now. Workaround: This issue happened because the memory configuration for Q7 was not tuned correctly resulting in faster buffer exhaustion. Customer should check memory allocations done for Q7 on the VSF link port.

description: Symptom: A drop occurs when packet(s) match an applied policy with PBR default-nexthop action and the LPM/longest prefix matching route in the VRF for that packet is a route type that copies to CPU (unresolved, nullroute, reject or interface). Scenario: This issue can occur in the following workflows: A policy with PBR action is applied to a Layer 3 interface in a given VRF in the routed-in direction and the selected/active entry from the PBR action list is of type default-nexthop. A packet ingresses the system on the same Layer 3 interface and the packet is matched by the qualifiers in the policy's IPv4 or IPv6 class. There exists one or more matching routes for the packet's destination address in the VRF and the longest prefix matching route (LPM) of that set is one of the following route types: + unresolved - the route's configured IPv4/IPv6 nexthop is currently unreachable + interface - the route is configured to egress matching traffic directly to another Layer 3 interface in the VRF, e.g. ROP, VLAN SVI or L3 LAG + nullroute - the route is configured to discard packets and return ICMP error to the sender + reject - the route is configured to discard packets silently This situation could occur randomly as dynamic routing topology changes introduce or remove routes of varying prefix lengths and nexthop states and/or the highest priority entry in the PBR action list changes type (e.g. default-nexthop to nexthop) depending on the changing reachability of the list entries. Workaround: Avoid using the PBR default-nexthop action entirely and use nexthop action instead, taking into account the fact that nexthop has higher priority and will redirect over any routing table entries in the VRF where the default-nexthop action has priority over the VRF's default route only, if one exists.

There exists one or more matching routes for the packet's destination address in the VRF and the longest prefix matching route (LPM) of that set is one of the following route types: + unresolved - the route's configured IPv4/IPv6 nexthop is currently unreachable + interface - the route is configured to egress matching traffic directly to another Layer 3 interface in the VRF, e.g. ROP, VLAN SVI or L3 LAG + nullroute - the route is configured to discard packets and return ICMP error to the sender + reject - the route is configured to discard packets silently This situation could occur randomly as dynamic routing topology changes introduce or remove routes of varying prefix lengths and nexthop states and/or the highest priority entry in the PBR action list changes type (e.g. default-nexthop to nexthop) depending on the changing reachability of the list entries. Workaround: Avoid using the PBR default-nexthop action entirely and use nexthop action instead, taking into account the fact that nexthop has higher priority and will redirect over any routing table entries in the VRF where the default-nexthop action has priority over the VRF's default route only, if one exists.

description: Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Because of a flaw in the way that port MAC addresses are assigned in the system, Switch #1's port 1/1/2 is assigned an identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or do not use both of the ports with the overlap.

description: Symptom: When Proxy ARP is configured, then the switch is responding to ARP request without validating ARP SRC IP. As a result, even though there is no reachability for ARP SRC IP in the switch, still it responds for ARP requests. Scenario: When an ARP request is received on an interface with Proxy ARP, then ARP response is sent without validating the ARP SRC IP. This is when there is a route to reach the destination but there is no reachability to ARP SRC IP.

description: Symptom: Customers see frequent log entries: HTTP <MAC-ADDRESS> - Invalid packet length or headers during normal HTTP traffic, even when no real packet corruption exists. No functional impact; just log noise. Scenario: This issue occurs on legitimate TCP packets to port 80 with no payload (ACK, SYN/ACK, FIN, keep-alive, window updates). Previously these were funneled into an error path (or indistinguishable from malformed cases), producing repeated ERR logs under moderate web or update traffic. Workaround: Disable the HTTP user-agent in device fingerprinting configuration.

description: Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Because of a flaw in the way that port MAC addresses are assigned in the swith, switch #1's port 1/1/2 will be assigned the identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two 6200, 6300, 6300L, 6400, 8100, or 8360 switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or don't use both of the ports with the overlap. Neither of those options is a feasible solution for a customer. Prior to the fix provided here, there is no explicit workaround for this issue.

description: Symptom: When wireless clients roam between access points connected to the same switch, they lose connectivity and receive an APIPA address. Connectivity is only restored after the MAC-age timer expires. Scenario: When a client is authenticated with a VLAN on a secure port, the VLAN is added as an ingress-only VLAN on other secure ports, but the MSTP state for those ports remains in blocked state instead of forwarding state, preventing seamless client movement. Workaround: Reduce the MAC-age timer to minimize the impact. or configure AP ports in device-mode instead of client-mode: interface 1/1/X port-access client-limit 1 port-access client-mode device.

description: Symptom: After a VSF system boot, isolated ports connected to clients experience complete traffic loss. Scenario: This issue can occur in a deployment with the following conditions: VSF has PVLAN configurations with an uplink LAG as promiscuous port. The LAG is made up of ports from VSF member 1 and member 2. For example, lag 1 with member ports 1/1/1 and 2/1/1 is the PVLAN promiscuous port. There are some of clients in a PVLAN- isolated VLAN connected to the VSF member 1 and member 2 physical ports.• After a VSF boot system, some of the clients are no longer reachable from when pinging through the uplink promiscuous port LAG• Workaround: On the LAG interface, use the command no private-VLAN port-type promiscuous to remove the promiscuous port type and then re-configure the setting with the command private-vlan port-type promiscuous.

There are some of clients in a PVLAN- isolated VLAN connected to the VSF member 1 and member 2 physical ports.• After a VSF boot system, some of the clients are no longer reachable from when pinging through the uplink promiscuous port LAG•
workaround: On the LAG interface, use the command no private-VLAN port-type promiscuous to remove the promiscuous port type and then re-configure the setting with the command private-vlan port-type promiscuous.

messages.content: What is the workaround for Bug 389860?

Private VLAN (Bug ID 389860): The documented workaround is: On the LAG interface, use the command no private-VLAN port-type promiscuous to remove the promiscuous port type and then re-configure the setting with the command private-vlan port-type promiscuous.

description: Symptom: The customer could see a system reboot on the VSF member with the reboot reason as critical service fault. The stack experienced a reboot due to failure of stack members to exchange heartbeat messages. Scenario: The customer could see this problem when they have a VSF stack device on the 6200 V1 platform switches. This issue is not reproducible consistently. Customer has seen this only once till now. Workaround: This issue happened because the memory configuration for Q7 was not tuned correctly resulting in faster buffer exhaustion. Customer should check memory allocations done for Q7 on the VSF link port.

description: Symptom: A drop occurs when packet(s) match an applied policy with PBR default-nexthop action and the LPM/longest prefix matching route in the VRF for that packet is a route type that copies to CPU (unresolved, nullroute, reject or interface). Scenario: This issue can occur in the following workflows: A policy with PBR action is applied to a Layer 3 interface in a given VRF in the routed-in direction and the selected/active entry from the PBR action list is of type default-nexthop. A packet ingresses the system on the same Layer 3 interface and the packet is matched by the qualifiers in the policy's IPv4 or IPv6 class. There exists one or more matching routes for the packet's destination address in the VRF and the longest prefix matching route (LPM) of that set is one of the following route types: + unresolved - the route's configured IPv4/IPv6 nexthop is currently unreachable + interface - the route is configured to egress matching traffic directly to another Layer 3 interface in the VRF, e.g. ROP, VLAN SVI or L3 LAG + nullroute - the route is configured to discard packets and return ICMP error to the sender + reject - the route is configured to discard packets silently This situation could occur randomly as dynamic routing topology changes introduce or remove routes of varying prefix lengths and nexthop states and/or the highest priority entry in the PBR action list changes type (e.g. default-nexthop to nexthop) depending on the changing reachability of the list entries. Workaround: Avoid using the PBR default-nexthop action entirely and use nexthop action instead, taking into account the fact that nexthop has higher priority and will redirect over any routing table entries in the VRF where the default-nexthop action has priority over the VRF's default route only, if one exists.
